utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: thanks , rachel . nice , nice to meet you .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: um , as my nurse told you , we're using dax . so i'm just gon na tell dax a little bit about you .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so rachel is a 48-year-old female here for shortness of breath . she has a history of depression , smoking , and chronic back pain . so tell me about this shortness of breath .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay . so there are times when i'm either doing very , very mild exercises or just walking , even if i'm just walking up , you know , my driveway , i find myself palpitating a lot , and there's a little bit of shortness of breath .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do n't know if it's got to do with the back pain , you know , whether that gets triggered as well at the same time .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: right .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: but definitely i feel it happens more often lately .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and anything else change recently ? like , have you changed lifestyle , like you're exercising more than you used to , having any allergies , anything like that ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: probably exercising more to get rid of the covid 15 .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: the covid 15 . yeah . now last time i saw you , you were smoking two packs a day . how much are you smoking now ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , it's gone down quite a bit because , yeah , we said we have to make some , you know , changes as you get older .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so i would say it's probably , um , maybe , maybe a couple ... probably a coup- i do n't know . probably once or day or something .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: just couple cigarettes a day ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: probably once a day , yeah .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: we're getting close .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that's awesome .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that's great news . um , and then how's your depression doing ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i have my moments .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: there are some days when i feel , you know , i wake up and everything was great .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: uh- .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: and then there are times , i do n't , i do n't know whether it's got to do with the weather or what else kind of triggers it .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: there are some days when i feel extremely low .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and you had been taking the effexor for your depression . are you still taking that ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes , i am .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , great . and then , um the chronic back pain , we've been giving you the gabapentin neurontin for that . is that helping control the pain ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i think it is .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it is ... it's definitely , um , i feel better .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: uh- .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: but it does come every now and then .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: right . what do you do when it's really bad ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , i try to just get as much rest as i can .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and you had talked about doing yoga . are you doing yoga anymore ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i wish i said yes , but i have n't really made it a habit .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . okay . well , um , you know , said ... you said you were coming in with shortness of breath , so we sent you to get some pulmonary function tests .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so let's just look at those . hey , dragon , show me the pulmonary function tests . okay , so it looks like ... , it's interesting . it says that you might be having a little bit of asthma or , uh , copd . and if you are , we'll talk about that .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: let's look at our x-ray . hey , dragon , show me the most recent x-ray . okay , i said it wrong . hey , dragon , show me the most recent chest x-ray . okay , this is interesting . your ... kind of your diaphragm is a little bit flatter , and we'll see that in some , uh , copd , which happens with smokers often . so let's just do a quick physical exam . i know my nurse did the review of systems with you . is there anything else bothering you that we need to talk about today ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no other issues .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . great . let's do the exam . all right , so your physical exam looks pretty normal other than you've got kind of these mild wheezes in all your lung fields . and so i think you do have copd from your pulmonary function tests , your x-ray , and that . so i'm gon na diagnose you with copd . chronic obstructive pulmonary disease . it means you're not able to exhale appropriately .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so we're gon na put you on a medicine called combivent . okay , you're gon na do two puffs twice a day . it's gon na help open up your lungs . it's an inhaler .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i'm also gon na prescribe albuterol , which you use when you get really short of breath . it's like a rescue thing .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: um , and then i'm gon na prescribe some steroids to help , also some prednisone . so let me just order those .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hey , dragon , order combivent , uh , two puffs twice a day . order albuterol , two puffs as needed . and order , uh , prednisone uh taper pack . okay , so and then it sounds like your depression's stable , so we're not gon na change anything . you're gon na keep taking the effexor . um , do yoga for depression and your back pain , so for your back pain , stay on the neurontin , and we just wo n't do anything different . any questions for me .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , i think this is good . thank you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: perfect . hey , dragon , finalize the note . why do n't you ...

detail_list.summary: The patient is a 48-year-old female with a history of depression, smoking, and chronic back pain.
Shortness of breath.
The patient reports shortness of breath and palpitations during mild exercise or walking.
The patient asks if her shortness of breath is related to her chronic back pain.
The doctor asks if the patient has changed her lifestyle, increased exercise, or has allergies.
The patient reports she has been exercising more.
The doctor asks about the patient's current smoking frequency.
The patient reports she has reduced smoking to approximately one cigarette per day.
Depression
The doctor asks how the patient's depression is doing.
The patient reports experiencing days where she feels extremely low.
The doctor asks if the patient is still taking Effexor.
The patient confirms she is still taking Effexor.
Back pain
The doctor asks if Neurontin is helping control the patient's back pain.
The patient reports that Neurontin is helping her feel better.
The doctor asks what the patient does when her back pain is severe.
The patient reports she tries to get as much rest as possible when her back pain is severe.
The doctor asks if the patient is still practicing yoga.
The patient reports she has not made yoga a habit.
Pulmonary function tests suggest asthma or COPD.
Chest X-ray shows flattening of the diaphragm, consistent with COPD.
The doctor asks if there are any other issues bothering the patient.
Physical exam shows mild wheezes bilaterally; the patient is diagnosed with COPD.
The doctor prescribes Combivent, 2 puffs twice a day.
The doctor prescribes albuterol, 2 puffs as needed.
The doctor prescribes a prednisone taper pack.
The patient will continue taking Effexor and Neurontin, and is encouraged to practice yoga for depression and back pain.

case_description: shortness of breath; smoking;depression;chronic back pain
